title: No graceful shutdown on process signals (SIGTERM, SIGINT, SIGHUP)
detail: The file sets up long-lived resources (chatManager with idleSweeper interval, StdioTransport, AgentServer) but has no signal handlers (process.on('SIGTERM'), process.on('SIGINT'), etc.). If the parent process sends SIGTERM or the user presses Ctrl+C, the process terminates immediately without calling AgentServer.close() to clean up (bgAgentBusUnsubscribe, chatManager.closeAll(), pending approvals, approval timers). This risks resource leaks and can leave child processes hanging (MCP servers, tool processes). The comment on line 170-171 incorrectly assumes shutdown is always graceful (stdin EOF only).
fix: Add signal handlers before the comment on line 170, e.g., `process.on('SIGTERM', () => { agentServer.close(); process.exit(0); })` and similarly for SIGINT and SIGHUP, ensuring cleanup before exit.
verify_note: This is a legitimate resource leak and process cleanup issue. The severity is high because: (1) the process is long-lived and may manage multiple concurrent sessions, (2) resources (pending approvals, timers, sessions, MCP server connections) are definitively leaked on abnormal termination, (3) child processes spawned by tools or MCP servers could remain orphaned. The fix is straightforward: store the agentServer instance, call chatManager.stopIdleSweeper(), and add signal handlers before the final comment block.
verify_reason: The file creates an AgentServer with chatManager containing an idleSweeper interval and establishes StdioTransport, but fails to register signal handlers (process.on for SIGTERM, SIGINT, SIGHUP). When the parent process sends SIGTERM or user presses Ctrl+C, the process terminates immediately without invoking agentServer.close(). The cleanup methods that should run (bgAgentBusUnsubscribe, chatManager.closeAll, pending approvals resolution, approval timers clearing, transport.close) are never called. Additionally, the agentServer instance is not even stored in a variable, making it impossible to call close() even if signal handlers were added. The comment on line 170-171 claiming "graceful" shutdown via stdin EOF is incorrect because the readline close event is never wired to trigger cleanup. Testing confirms the process exits with code 143 (SIGTERM) without calling any cleanup methods.

title: Race condition in async setInterval callback with overlapping executions
detail: The async callback in setInterval (line 100) can overlap if onExecute takes longer than intervalMs. Multiple timer firings will queue simultaneously, causing nextRun and runCount to be updated multiple times before the first execution completes. The calculation on line 104 (nextRun = Date.now() + intervalMs) will then be wrong for all but the last queued execution. For long-running jobs, this causes scheduling drift and incorrect state tracking.
fix: Use a flag to prevent concurrent executions (e.g., set a boolean flag before awaiting, clear after), or use setTimeout with manual rescheduling instead of setInterval to ensure sequential execution.
verify_note: This is a genuine race condition that affects the core scheduling semantics. While it doesn't prevent execution, it breaks the timing guarantees and state tracking that users rely on. The fix suggested by the reviewer (using a flag to prevent concurrent executions, or switching to setTimeout with manual rescheduling) is appropriate. This should be prioritized as it affects reliability of the cron scheduler in production.
verify_reason: The setInterval callback can indeed be queued multiple times if onExecute takes longer than intervalMs. JavaScript's setInterval fires independently of callback completion. When multiple timer firings occur before the first onExecute completes, the synchronous state updates (lines 102-104) execute multiple times before any await. This overwrites job.lastRun, job.nextRun, and increments job.runCount multiple times. The nextRun calculation becomes incorrect because it will reflect only the last timer firing time, not the actual scheduled next execution. For example, if a job runs every 5 seconds but onExecute takes 10 seconds, the timer fires at 0ms, 5000ms, and 10000ms. The state updates at 5000ms and 10000ms overwrite earlier values, causing nextRun to drift forward incorrectly.

title: File cache invalidated with relative paths instead of absolute paths
detail: At lines 109-111, fileCache.invalidate() is called with hunk.path and hunk.movePath (relative paths from the patch). However, at line 102, applyPatch() is called with parsed.hunks containing the original relative paths. The applier resolves these to absolute paths internally (line 92 in applier.ts via resolveAgainst()). If fileCache stores entries by absolute path, invalidating by relative path will not match and cache entries will remain stale. This could cause the tool to report file contents that don't reflect the patch application.
fix: Store the resolved absolute paths from the planning phase and invalidate by those absolute paths, or pass the resolved paths back from applyPatch() in the result object.
verify_note: This is a real correctness bug. If a tool applies a patch and then immediately reads the same file, the Read tool will get the old cached content instead of the modified content (assuming the file hasn't changed on disk according to mtime, which it recently did). The severity is high because this directly violates cache coherency - patches modify files but the cache doesn't reflect the modifications.
verify_reason: The fileCache is keyed by absolute paths (see file-cache.ts and usage in edit.ts line 80, read.ts lines 64-67). However, in apply-patch/index.ts lines 109-111, fileCache.invalidate() is called with relative paths (hunk.path and hunk.movePath) from parsed.hunks. These relative paths will not match the absolute path keys stored in fileCache. The applyPatch() function in applier.ts internally resolves relative paths to absolute paths (line 92: resolveAgainst()) and modifies files at those absolute locations, but returns ApplyPatchResult with relative paths (lines 101, 117, 151, 158 push `original` which is the relative path). Therefore, the cache invalidation at lines 109-111 will fail to match cache entries, leaving stale cached file content that doesn't reflect the patch application.

title: Command injection vulnerability via unsanitized git diff argument
detail: The `_arg` parameter (user input) is interpolated directly into shell commands without escaping: `execSync(\`git diff --stat HEAD ${file}\`, ...)`. An attacker could pass shell metacharacters like `; rm -rf /` or backticks to execute arbitrary commands. This affects both the stat and diff commands at these lines.
fix: Use `execFileSync` instead of `execSync` to avoid shell interpretation, or properly escape the file argument using a library like `shell-escape` before interpolation.
verify_note: This is a clear command injection vulnerability affecting both lines 512 and 523 in the /diff command. The user input from the command dispatcher (registry.ts line 108) flows directly into shell command execution without any constraints or sanitization. The fix should use execFileSync instead of execSync, or properly escape file arguments using proper escaping mechanisms before interpolation.
verify_reason: The /diff command in core-commands.ts (lines 512 and 523) directly interpolates the user-provided `_arg` parameter into shell command templates without any sanitization or escaping. The code `execSync(\`git diff --stat HEAD ${file}\`, ...)` passes a backtick template literal to execSync, which interprets it as a shell command. This allows an attacker to inject shell metacharacters like `; rm -rf /` or backticks to execute arbitrary commands. Tested locally: passing `file.txt; touch /tmp/pwned.txt #` successfully creates /tmp/pwned.txt despite the git command failing, confirming command injection works.

title: Root position TOP inset resolved against wrong dimension
detail: Line 958 resolves position TOP inset using `isDefined(w) ? w : 0` (width), but should use height. Top position insets should resolve against the container's height dimension, not width. This causes percentage-based position:top values on the root to be calculated incorrectly when the owner width differs significantly from height.
fix: Change line 958 from `isDefined(w) ? w : 0` to `isDefined(h) ? h : 0`
verify_note: This is a clear defect that will cause percentage-based position:top calculations to be incorrect when applied to root nodes. For example, if a root node has `position: top: 50%` and the container has width=800 height=600, the top offset would be calculated as 50% of 800 (400px) instead of 50% of 600 (300px). This is a functional correctness bug.
verify_reason: Line 958 uses `isDefined(w) ? w : 0` to resolve the TOP position inset, but this is incorrect. The variable `w` holds the owner width, while `h` (defined on line 937) holds the owner height. For percentage-based position values, TOP/BOTTOM insets should resolve against the container's height dimension, not width. This is a straightforward copy-paste error from line 954 (which correctly uses width for LEFT position). The fix is to change line 958 from `isDefined(w) ? w : 0` to `isDefined(h) ? h : 0`.

title: Flex item relative position TOP/BOTTOM resolved against wrong dimension
detail: Lines 1861 and 1865 resolve position TOP and BOTTOM insets using `ownerW` (width), but should use `ownerH` (height). Percentage-based position:top and position:bottom values on flex items with relative positioning will be calculated incorrectly, causing items to be positioned at wrong vertical offsets when using percentage position values.
fix: Change line 1861 from `ownerW` to `ownerH` and line 1865 from `ownerW` to `ownerH`
verify_note: This is a clear and demonstrable bug confirmed by comparison with the correct absolute positioning logic in the same file. The fix is straightforward: change lines 1861 and 1865 from `ownerW` to `ownerH`.
verify_reason: Lines 1861 and 1865 in the flex layout relative positioning section incorrectly use `ownerW` (parent width) when resolving percentage values for `EDGE_TOP` and `EDGE_BOTTOM`. According to CSS specifications, TOP and BOTTOM insets should resolve against the parent's HEIGHT dimension, not width. This is evident from the absolute positioning implementation in `layoutAbsoluteChild()` (lines 1918-1921), which correctly uses `parentHeight` for TOP and BOTTOM resolution. The flex layout code should use `ownerH` at lines 1861 and 1865 instead of `ownerW`. This causes percentage-based vertical position offsets on relative-positioned flex items to be calculated against the wrong dimension, resulting in incorrect vertical positioning.

title: Missing await on async tool function calls
detail: The executeIterateWebTool function calls webSearchTool(args) and webFetchTool(args) without awaiting them. Both functions are async and return Promise<string>, so the current code returns Promise<Promise<string>> instead of Promise<string>. This breaks the contract expected by callers in argue.ts line 162, which do `await executeIterateWebTool(...)` expecting a string result. The Promise will resolve to an unawaited Promise, causing tool results to be malformed.
fix: Add await before both webSearchTool(args) on line 53 and webFetchTool(args) on line 56: `return await webSearchTool(args);` and `return await webFetchTool(args);`
verify_note: While this is a real defect that violates the explicit return type, it may not cause actual runtime failures due to Promise flattening behavior in async functions. However, it's semantically incorrect and could cause subtle issues if the code is refactored. With strict TypeScript enabled (tsconfig shows "strict": true), the compiler should detect this as a type violation.
verify_reason: The executeIterateWebTool function (lines 48-59 in web-tools.ts) is declared as async and returns Promise<string>. However, lines 53 and 56 call webSearchTool(args) and webFetchTool(args) without awaiting them. Both of these are async functions explicitly typed as returning Promise<string> (web-search.ts line 107, web-fetch.ts line 185). Without await, the code returns the Promise object directly without waiting for it to resolve. While JavaScript's implicit Promise flattening in async functions may cause this to accidentally work at runtime (the Promise gets unwrapped one level), this violates the explicit type contract and is semantically incorrect. The calls should be: `return await webSearchTool(args);` and `return await webFetchTool(args);`

title: LLM not invoked after tool execution on final round
detail: If tools are executed on the last allowed round (round === maxRounds), the tool results are appended to messages (line 136) but the loop then exits without making another LLM call to generate final findings. The finalText is set on line 140 to response.text from the response that contained tool calls, which may not include final output. This leaves the LLM unable to synthesize findings after the last tool round.
fix: Either: (1) use `round < maxRounds` instead of `round <= maxRounds` and make an additional forced-conclude call after the loop if needed, or (2) add a force-conclude path after the loop similar to participant-research.ts if finalText is empty
verify_note: This is a real defect but the severity may be medium rather than high because: (1) it only occurs if tools are executed on the final allowed round, (2) the intermediate response text may contain partial useful information, and (3) the tool results are captured in the message history even if not processed. However, it does prevent the LLM from synthesizing final findings after tool execution, which is the stated design goal of the expansion phase.
verify_reason: In planning-detail-expansion.ts, the loop exits after executing tools on the final round (round === maxRounds) without giving the LLM a chance to synthesize results. Lines 127-136 append tool results to messages, but line 140 sets finalText to response.text from the response that contained tool_use blocks, not a final synthesis. Unlike participant-research.ts which has a force-conclude mechanism (lines 195-226) to make an additional LLM call when finalText is empty, planning-detail-expansion.ts has no such recovery. The LLM never processes the tool results appended on the final round, leaving finalText populated with intermediate response text rather than final findings.

title: Running git commands on already-removed worktree will fail
detail: After line 89 removes the worktree with `--force`, the removeWorktree function attempts to run `git branch --show-current` at line 97 with `cwd: worktreePath`. Since the worktree directory was just deleted, this git command will fail (no .git/worktrees directory). The try-catch silently swallows this, but the branch is never deleted when removeBranch=true.
fix: Extract and store the branch name before removing the worktree (line 89), then use it to delete the branch. For example, get the branch name from findGitRoot and git branch queries before the worktree is removed.
verify_note: This is a correctness bug that causes git branch references to leak when removeBranch=true. The application won't crash due to the try-catch, but the cleanup is incomplete. The proper fix would be to query the branch name before removing the worktree (line 89) and store it, or use git worktree list to extract it beforehand.
verify_reason: The code removes the worktree directory at line 89 with `git worktree remove worktreePath --force`. Then at lines 97-101, it attempts to run `git branch --show-current` with `cwd: worktreePath`. Since the worktree directory no longer exists after removal, execFileSync will fail when attempting to set the current working directory. The nested try-catch (lines 96-107) catches this error silently, preventing the branch deletion code at line 103 from ever executing. This means when removeBranch=true, the branch is never actually deleted due to the failed git command in a non-existent directory.

title: Race condition: polling for server readiness without guarantee
detail: In getClient(), when state is 'starting', the code waits exactly 2 seconds with `await new Promise((r) => setTimeout(r, 2000))` then returns managed.client. However, if initialization takes longer than 2 seconds, the client may still be initializing or not yet set. Additionally, if called concurrently twice, both threads may enter the 'starting' state branch and both sleep, returning potentially uninitialized clients. There's no proper synchronization mechanism or state polling that checks if the client actually reached 'ready' state.
fix: Use a proper synchronization primitive (e.g., a promise that resolves when state changes to 'ready') rather than a fixed sleep. Or: keep looping with backoff until state === 'ready', with a reasonable timeout and max retries.
verify_note: This is a real correctness bug. Severity is marked medium rather than high because: (1) the bug only manifests under concurrent access patterns, (2) most LSP server initialization completes within 2 seconds in practice, (3) callers check for undefined clients and return errors (line 57-58 in lsp.ts), (4) the 30-second request timeout on LSP operations (client.ts line 96-101) would eventually fail cleanly rather than hang. The fix would be to replace the fixed sleep with proper synchronization: either a Promise that resolves when state changes to 'ready', or a polling loop checking state with backoff and timeout.
verify_reason: The code has a genuine race condition in the `getClient()` method. When `state === "starting"` (line 44), the code waits exactly 2 seconds with `await new Promise((r) => setTimeout(r, 2000))` then returns `managed.client` without verifying initialization actually completed. If concurrent calls occur: (1) Call A enters startServer(), sets state to "starting", begins async initialization, (2) Call B immediately sees state "starting" and sleeps for 2 seconds, (3) If Call A's initialization takes longer than 2 seconds, Call B wakes and returns `managed.client` which may still be undefined or uninitialized. Additionally, `managed.client` is assigned at line 75 only after both start() and initialize() complete at lines 72-73, but a concurrent waiter could return before this assignment. The fixed 2-second sleep provides no guarantee that initialization finished — it's a time-based sleep, not a state-based synchronization primitive.

title: Inconsistent state if reinstall fails after uninstall
detail: Line 45 calls uninstallPluginByName(name) which removes the plugin directory. If line 46's installPluginFromPath() throws an error, the plugin is already deleted and cannot be recovered, leaving the system in an inconsistent state where the user's plugin is completely lost.
fix: Wrap the uninstall+install sequence in try-catch, or call install first (to validate) before uninstalling, or add rollback logic to re-install on failure.
verify_note: This is a real defect but not high severity. Recovery is possible by re-running the update command (which would reinstall) or explicitly reinstalling the plugin, and the source code is never deleted. However, the inconsistent state (missing metadata) could confuse users or cause issues if the system state is queried before recovery. The fix recommended in the finding (try-catch with rollback, or validate-before-delete) would improve reliability.
verify_reason: Lines 61-62 in update.ts perform uninstall and then install without atomicity. If uninstallPluginByName(name) succeeds but installPluginFromPath() throws an error, the plugin directory and metadata are already deleted. While installPluginFromPath has its own try-catch to clean up the temporary build directory, the earlier uninstall call has already committed the deletion. This leaves the system in an inconsistent state: the plugin's .cs-meta.json and registry entries are gone, though the source directory remains intact. The error is not silently swallowed—it propagates to the caller—but there is no rollback mechanism to restore the deleted metadata.

title: Logic error: exit handler always rejects due to redundant condition
detail: At line 54, this.connected is set to false. At line 55, the condition if (!this.connected) will always be true, so the rejection always executes regardless of whether the process exited during initial connection or after successful connection. The intent appears to be checking if we successfully connected before the exit event, but the logic is inverted.
fix: Change the exit handler to track connection state separately or check the exit code. For example, reject only if not already connected: if (!this.connected && !this.ssh?.killed) { reject(...) }
verify_note: This is a real logic error, but its practical impact is mitigated by the fact that the Promise is likely already resolved by line 45 (when stdout data arrives), so the reject() call at line 57 becomes a no-op. However, this is fragile - if the code is refactored or if exit events fire before data events, this could cause unexpected rejections. The condition should be: move the if check before the assignment, or check this.connected before modifying it.
verify_reason: The exit handler at lines 53-59 has a clear logic error. Line 54 sets this.connected to false, and then line 55 checks if (!this.connected), which will always be true. This means the rejection always executes when the exit event fires, regardless of whether connection was successful. While the Promise may already be settled (resolved) by the time exit fires, making the reject() call a no-op in practice, this is a genuine logic error and code smell. The condition is redundant/illogical - it should check this.connected BEFORE modifying it, not after. The condition at line 55 should either be removed entirely (if rejection on all exits is intended) or should check the state before line 54 modifies it.

title: Promise rejection race condition: error events after resolution are unhandled
detail: The promise is resolved at line 45 when stdout data arrives. However, subsequent error events (line 48-51) or exit events (line 53-59) can still fire and attempt to reject an already-resolved promise. The reject on line 50 will be a no-op for a resolved promise, but it may trigger unhandled rejection warnings. A flag should track whether the promise has already resolved.
fix: Add a resolved flag: let resolved = false; Set it to true after resolve/reject, then check it before executing reject: if (!resolved) { resolved = true; reject(err); }
verify_note: This is a real defect but with mitigated practical impact. JavaScript's promise implementation safely ignores rejection attempts on already-resolved promises, so no unhandled rejection will be thrown. However, it represents poor practice: event listeners remain attached after promise settlement (memory leak risk), and the reject logic at line 55 contains a logic error (always-true condition). The suggested fix (adding a resolved flag) is appropriate and would clarify intent while ensuring only one settlement path executes. Severity is medium rather than high because the code won't crash, but it should be fixed for maintainability and to prevent potential future bugs.
verify_reason: The code has a genuine race condition. Line 42 uses `.once()` to register a single data listener that resolves the promise. Lines 48-51 and 53-59 use `.on()` to register persistent error and exit listeners. If either the error or exit event fires AFTER the promise has resolved (via the data event), the reject() calls will attempt to reject an already-resolved promise. While JavaScript silently ignores this (no-op), it indicates poor error handling: event listeners are never cleaned up after promise settlement, creating a maintenance issue. Additionally, line 55 has a logic bug: it checks `if (!this.connected)` immediately after setting `this.connected = false`, so this condition always evaluates to true, meaning the process always tries to reject on exit regardless of the intended logic.

title: Command injection vulnerability in macOS notification
detail: The URL constructed with escape(message) and escape(title) is passed directly to osascript via execSync with double quotes. The escape() function replaces quotes and backslashes, but when placed inside a double-quoted string in the shell command, malicious input like `$(command)` or backtick substitution could still execute arbitrary commands. Example: a title containing `$(whoami)` would execute despite escaping.
fix: Use parameterized execution or properly quote the entire osascript command. Consider using 'printf %q' for shell-safe quoting or pass arguments via environment variables instead of string interpolation.
verify_note: The reviewer's specific example of $(whoami) would NOT actually execute because command substitution doesn't occur inside single quotes. However, the underlying vulnerability is real: single quotes in the input CAN break the shell quoting. The practical risk in the current codebase is lower than the severity rating suggests because notifyComplete() and notifyError() only pass controlled strings to notify(). But the direct notify() function is exported and could be misused with untrusted input. The fix should use printf %q for proper shell quoting or avoid shell execution entirely.
verify_reason: The escape() function does not properly protect against shell injection via single quotes. In shell syntax, backslash does not escape quotes inside single-quoted strings — a backslash before a quote just produces a literal backslash and quote, which breaks the quoting boundary. Therefore, if a message or title contains a single quote, the escaped output like `test\'` will break out of the single-quoted osascript command, allowing arbitrary shell command injection. For example, a message containing `'; whoami; '` would result in the shell executing the whoami command as a separate statement.

title: __agentName is read from args but never injected
detail: Line 33 reads `const from = (args.__agentName as string) ?? "main";` but `__agentName` is never injected into args anywhere in the codebase (verified via grep across the entire tool-system and executor). This means all SendMessage tool calls always use the fallback value "main" for the `from` field, even when the tool is called from a named sub-agent. The agentCoordinator.send() method at line 49 will record the message as coming from "main" regardless of which agent actually called the tool, breaking agent-to-agent message attribution.
fix: Add an `agentName` field to ToolContext (or use a different mechanism to inject the agent's identity into args), and have ToolRegistry inject the current agent name into args when executing the SendMessage tool, similar to how `__signal` is injected at line 118 of registry.ts.
verify_note: This is a real defect but the entire multi-agent messaging feature appears incomplete - agent registration is also missing. The feature is marked as Phase 4 (comment in builtin/index.ts) and may still be under active development. The defect should be fixed together with proper agent registration in the coordinator.
verify_reason: The `__agentName` parameter is read at line 33 of send-message.ts (`const from = (args.__agentName as string) ?? "main";`) but is never injected into args anywhere in the codebase. A codebase-wide search confirms: (1) no injection mechanism exists in registry.ts or elsewhere, (2) the pattern exists for `__signal` (injected at registry.ts:118) but not for `__agentName`, (3) `agentCoordinator.register()` is never called anywhere to register agents. This means SendMessage always records messages as coming from "main" instead of the actual calling agent.

title: Cached log path doesn't roll over at midnight
detail: The `logPath()` function caches the result of `todayLogPath()` in the module-scoped `_path` variable (line 51). Once cached (line 54), it is never re-evaluated, even if the date changes to a new day. A call to `dlog()` after midnight will continue writing to yesterday's log file instead of creating a new daily log for today, violating the intended daily-rollover behavior documented in the file header.
fix: Check if the cached date still matches today before returning the cached path. Compare the logged date from `todayLogPath()` with `_path` on each call, or use a time-based cache invalidation.
verify_note: Real defect affecting the documented daily-rollover behavior. Severity is medium rather than high because: (1) it only affects long-running sessions spanning midnight, which may be rare in desktop app usage; (2) it doesn't cause data loss or security issues, just improper log file organization; (3) logs are still written, just to the wrong file. The fix is straightforward: cache the date alongside the path and re-evaluate when the date changes, or call `todayLogPath()` on each invocation since it's not expensive.
verify_reason: The `_path` variable is set to `todayLogPath()` once on first call (line 54) and never re-evaluated. On subsequent calls, the function returns the cached value unconditionally (line 62). If the Electron app runs past midnight without restart, subsequent `dlog()` calls will continue appending to yesterday's log file path instead of rolling over to today's log. The `todayLogPath()` function correctly generates the date-based filename (lines 43-48), but this is only called once due to the check on line 53 (`if (_path === null)`). After midnight, `_path` is no longer null, so `todayLogPath()` is never re-invoked, preventing the daily rollover documented in the file header.

title: RPC function has no error handling or timeout, causes infinite hangs
detail: The `rpc()` function creates a Promise that only resolves via callback in `pending` map (line 73), but has no rejection, timeout, or error handling. If `ipcRenderer.send()` fails or if the main process never responds, the promise hangs indefinitely. The callback is never invoked if no response arrives.
fix: Add error handling: wrap `ipcRenderer.send()` in try-catch, add a timeout using Promise.race() with a timeout promise that rejects, and ensure cleanup of the pending callback on rejection.
verify_note: This is real but with lower severity than initially stated. The protocol design (protocol/server.ts and protocol/transport.ts) ensures responses ARE reliably sent in normal operation. However, the preload's rpc() function lacks defensive error handling (no try-catch around ipcRenderer.send(), no timeout, no rejection path). A hung worker that sends no response would cause the Promise to hang indefinitely, blocking the UI. The risk is primarily for edge cases (worker hanging without crashing, or transient IPC failures) rather than normal operation. The vulnerability could be fixed by: (1) wrapping ipcRenderer.send() in try-catch, (2) adding Promise.race() with a timeout, (3) cleaning up the pending callback on rejection. Worker crashes are partially mitigated by agent-bridge.ts lifecycle events and App.tsx's .catch() handler (line 693), but a silent hang is not caught.
verify_reason: The rpc() function in packages/desktop/src/preload/index.ts (lines 69-76) creates a Promise that only resolves via a callback in the pending map (line 73). While the RPC protocol design ensures responses are sent by the server (protocol/server.ts shows every handler sends either success or error response), there is no timeout or rejection path in the preload's rpc() function itself. If ipcRenderer.send() fails silently, if the worker process hangs without crashing, or if the main process silently loses the message, the Promise would hang indefinitely. The .catch() handler in App.tsx (line 693) only catches rejections that reach the renderer, but a hung Promise that never resolves also never rejects, leaving the UI stuck in busy state without feedback. While the protocol layer provides strong guarantees for normal paths, the preload function itself lacks defensive mechanisms (timeout, try-catch around send, rejection handling).

title: File paths passed to main process without traversal validation
detail: Multiple functions (`readSkillBody` line 213, `readAgentBody` line 223, and others) accept file paths and pass them directly to main process handlers without validating they stay within expected directories. An attacker can pass paths like '../../../etc/passwd' or absolute paths to read arbitrary files.
fix: Validate paths: reject absolute paths, reject paths containing '..', and ensure paths are relative to expected base directories before sending to main process.
verify_note: While the vulnerability is real and the code lacks proper validation, the practical risk is somewhat mitigated by: (1) context isolation is enabled in the Electron configuration, (2) the preload uses contextBridge which creates a controlled API surface, (3) in the current codebase, readSkillBody is only called in PluginsAndSkillsSection.tsx with filePaths from listSkills which scans legitimate directories. The severity would be HIGH if there's a separate renderer XSS or malicious plugin vulnerability that could pass arbitrary paths, but as a standalone issue it's MEDIUM because the attack requires additional compromises to exploit. The fix is straightforward: apply the same path validation used in uninstallSkill to readSkillBody and readAgentBody.
verify_reason: The functions `readSkillBody` (line 46-48 of skills-service.ts) and `readAgentBody` (line 56-58 of agents-service.ts) accept file paths and directly pass them to `fs.readFile()` without any validation. The IPC handlers in main/index.ts (line 273 for skills:read and line 293-295 for agents:read) also lack path validation - they only perform type checking. This creates a path traversal vulnerability where if an attacker can control the filePath parameter (through renderer compromise, plugin injection, or other attack vectors), they can read arbitrary files on the system that the Electron process has access to. In contrast, the `uninstallSkill` function (line 56-79 of skills-service.ts) demonstrates proper validation with path endpoint checks, ".." rejection, and root containment verification - these safeguards are entirely absent from the read functions.

title: Unhandled promise rejection in load() function
detail: The load() async function calls window.codeshell.getSettings() without wrapping it in a try-catch block (lines 27-33). If the promise rejects, it will trigger an unhandled rejection. Compare to ModelSection.tsx line 228-235 which properly wraps async calls in try-catch.
fix: Wrap the getSettings call in try-catch: try { const s = (await window.codeshell.getSettings(scope, cwd)) ?? {}; ... } catch (e) { console.error(e); }
verify_note: This is a real defect that will cause unhandled promise rejections if getSettings fails (IPC error, service unavailable, etc). While severity is medium because it requires a failure condition and won't crash the app, it's still problematic because: (1) other similar sections (ModelSection) handle it correctly, indicating this is oversight; (2) unhandled rejections are logged as errors/warnings; (3) the pattern can lead to silent failures where the UI fails to load settings without user feedback. The proposed fix (wrap in try-catch and use setError state) matches the established pattern in ModelSection.
verify_reason: The load() function in PermissionSection.tsx (lines 27-33) calls window.codeshell.getSettings() which is backed by ipcRenderer.invoke() (preload/index.ts:196). The ipcRenderer.invoke() API returns a Promise that can reject if the IPC call fails. Since load() has no try-catch block and is called via void load() in useEffect (line 36), any rejection will be an unhandled promise rejection. This is confirmed by comparing to ModelSection.tsx (lines 228-235) which correctly wraps the same getSettings call in try-catch and stores errors in component state via setError().

title: Command injection vulnerability in /files command pattern
detail: The `pattern` variable from user input is directly interpolated into a shell command without escaping: `find . -maxdepth 3 -name "${pattern}"`. An attacker can inject arbitrary shell commands using backticks or `$(...)` syntax, e.g., `/files $(rm -rf /)` would execute the rm command.
fix: Use execSync's built-in argument arrays instead of string interpolation, or escape the pattern using shell escaping library (e.g., `shlex.quote` equivalent) before interpolating.
verify_note: This is a real command injection vulnerability. However, severity is medium rather than high because: (1) it's a local vulnerability in a TUI application where the user is actively typing commands; (2) it affects only the user's own machine context; (3) it's not a remote or network-exposed injection vector. The proper fix is to use execSync's array-based argument passing (e.g., execSync('find', ['.', '-maxdepth', '3', '-name', pattern, ...])) or use a shell escaping library before string interpolation.
verify_reason: The pattern variable at line 18 comes directly from untrimmed user input (`_arg.trim()`) with no sanitization. It is then interpolated directly into a shell command string at line 21: `find . -maxdepth 3 -name "${pattern}"`. The command is executed via execSync which interprets shell metacharacters. Shell command injection via $(...) or backticks is possible: e.g., `/files $(rm /tmp/test)` would execute the rm command before find sees the pattern.

title: Promise in waitUntilExit can hang indefinitely if unmount is called first
detail: resolveExitPromise and rejectExitPromise are initialized as no-ops (lines 410-411). In unmount (lines 1757-1759), these are called to settle the promise. However, if unmount is called before waitUntilExit is ever called, the no-ops execute and do nothing. When waitUntilExit is later called (lines 1762-1768), it creates a NEW promise but unmount's promise settlement (already executed) had no effect. The new promise will never be resolved/rejected because unmount has already finished and will not be called again.
fix: In waitUntilExit, ensure the promise is created first before assigning resolve/reject handlers, or move the promise creation/handler assignment to the constructor. Alternatively, check if unmount has already been called and resolve/reject synchronously in waitUntilExit.
verify_note: This is a real defect, but its impact depends on usage patterns. In normal operation (ui/index.tsx line 92), waitUntilExit() is called immediately after render(), so the issue doesn't manifest. However, if user code explicitly calls instance.unmount() before awaiting instance.waitUntilExit(), the promise will hang indefinitely. The fix would be to either: (1) check isUnmounted in waitUntilExit() and settle the promise synchronously, or (2) capture the resolve/reject handlers before unmount is called, or (3) ensure the promise is created in the constructor.
verify_reason: The code initializes resolveExitPromise and rejectExitPromise as no-ops (lines 410-411). If unmount() is called before waitUntilExit(), the no-ops execute without effect. When waitUntilExit() is later called (line 1762-1768), the ||= operator creates a NEW promise and assigns new resolve/reject handlers. However, since unmount() has already run and sets isUnmounted to true (line 1736), it will never run again (due to the guard at line 1684-1686). Therefore, the new promise will never be settled, causing waitUntilExit() to hang indefinitely.

title: Text truncation ignores ANSI color codes, corrupting output
detail: Line 46 uses `text.substring(0, borderLength)` to truncate text that may contain ANSI color codes (as stated in the BorderTextOptions comment on line 10: 'Pre-rendered string with ANSI color codes'). Substring truncates by character count, not visual width, which will split ANSI escape sequences and produce invalid output. The function should use `stringWidth` (available at line 6) to ensure truncation respects visual width like the rest of the function does.
fix: Use a width-aware truncation: iterate through the text character-by-character, tracking visual width via `stringWidth`, and truncate at the appropriate point to match `borderLength` visually.
verify_note: Real bug with moderate severity. It only triggers in the edge case where text visual width exceeds border width (line 45 condition), but when it does occur, the output will be visually corrupted due to broken ANSI escape sequences. The codebase already has the correct solution available in truncateToWidthNoEllipsis; using that would fix this issue.
verify_reason: At line 46, `text.substring(0, borderLength)` truncates by character count, not visual width. The text parameter can contain ANSI color codes (as documented in the BorderTextOptions type at line 10). When ANSI codes are present, taking the first N characters will likely: (1) cut mid-ANSI-sequence if codes are embedded throughout the text, producing invalid escape sequences; (2) result in truncated output that doesn't reach the intended borderLength visual width. The codebase has width-aware truncation utilities available (truncateToWidthNoEllipsis in packages/tui/src/ui/truncate.ts, lines 108-123) that properly handle ANSI codes using stringWidth and grapheme segmentation, but they are not used here.

title: Cursor movement 'l' can position cursor at -1 in empty text
detail: Line 108 does: s.cursor = Math.min(text.length - 1, s.cursor + 1). When text is empty (length 0), this evaluates to Math.min(-1, s.cursor + 1), which can be -1 if s.cursor is also < -1. Even worse, this allows cursor to reach -1, violating the invariant that cursor >= 0. The clamping should handle the empty-text case explicitly.
fix: Change to: s.cursor = text.length > 0 ? Math.min(text.length - 1, s.cursor + 1) : 0;
verify_note: While the bug is real, the practical impact is somewhat mitigated because JavaScript's negative array indices used in subsequent operations (like text.slice()) have defined semantics that work backwards from the end. However, it still violates a clear invariant that should be maintained. The severity is medium rather than high because: (1) empty text is an edge case, (2) operations using the -1 cursor value still function (albeit with wrong semantics), and (3) the bug requires a specific interaction pattern (pressing 'l' in normal/visual mode with empty text). Fix is straightforward: check `text.length > 0` before moving the cursor rightward.
verify_reason: Line 108 executes `s.cursor = Math.min(text.length - 1, s.cursor + 1)`. When text is empty (length 0), this evaluates to `Math.min(-1, s.cursor + 1)`. Since cursor is always initialized to 0 and guarded to stay >= 0 throughout normal operations, pressing 'l' with empty text results in `Math.min(-1, 1)` = `-1`, violating the invariant that cursor >= 0. This is inconsistent with other navigation commands like 'h' (line 105: `Math.max(0, ...)`) and '$' (line 114: `Math.max(0, text.length - 1)`) which properly guard against negative values. The same bug exists on line 174 for the 'l' command in visual mode.

title: Cursor movement 'l' in visual mode can position cursor at -1
detail: Line 174 in handleVisualMode has the same issue as line 108: Math.min(text.length - 1, s.cursor + 1) when text is empty (length 0) evaluates to Math.min(-1, ...), allowing cursor to reach -1. This violates the cursor >= 0 invariant.
fix: Change to: s.cursor = text.length > 0 ? Math.min(text.length - 1, s.cursor + 1) : 0;
verify_note: This is a genuine correctness defect. The bug can manifest when handling empty text in visual mode. While the practical impact depends on how the cursor value is used downstream (could cause array indexing issues or undefined behavior), the invariant violation is real and reproducible. The proposed fix is correct and necessary.
verify_reason: Line 174 uses `Math.min(text.length - 1, s.cursor + 1)`. When text is empty (length 0), text.length - 1 equals -1. With cursor at 0, this evaluates to Math.min(-1, 1) = -1, violating the cursor >= 0 invariant. This can occur when entering visual mode with empty text and pressing 'l' to move right. The vulnerability is not guarded by any bounds checks in the codebase.

title: Incorrect logic in probeTmuxControlModeSync: env heuristic result cached as probe result
detail: At line 35, the function assigns isTmuxControlModeEnvHeuristic() to tmuxControlModeProbed and returns early if true (line 36). This means when the heuristic detects control mode, it never actually probes tmux, it just caches the heuristic result. More critically, when heuristic is false, it continues but the cached value is false, then overwrites it with the actual probe result. The logic conflates 'env heuristic said yes' with 'we probed tmux and confirmed'. If heuristic returns true, we should cache true without further probing. If it returns false, we should probe and cache the probe result. Currently line 35 caches the heuristic result, not a probe result.
fix: Remove lines 35-36. Start probing directly only if TERM_PROGRAM is not set or not iTerm (the probe's purpose). Store the probe result in tmuxControlModeProbed at line 50.
verify_note: The severity is medium rather than high because: (1) The bug only manifests in the specific edge case of non-iTerm TERM_PROGRAM values in tmux with actual control mode enabled, which is relatively rare. (2) The heuristic correctly handles the primary iTerm2 control mode case. (3) Normal tmux usage (where TERM_PROGRAM is unset or set to tmux) still probes correctly. However, the logic is definitively incorrect and should be fixed to separate heuristic detection from actual probe results.
verify_reason: The logic has two problems: (1) Line 35 assigns the heuristic result (not a probed result) to `tmuxControlModeProbed`, creating semantic confusion—the variable name implies a probed value but receives a heuristic value. (2) Line 38's condition `if (process.env.TERM_PROGRAM) return` is overly broad: it skips probing whenever TERM_PROGRAM is set to anything, even if it's not iTerm. This means if you're in tmux with TERM_PROGRAM set to something like "Apple_Terminal", the function returns false without actually probing tmux, potentially missing control mode. The probe should only be skipped if probing is impossible or unnecessary, not whenever TERM_PROGRAM is defined.

title: Uninitialized finalText passed to parser on max-token failure
detail: When a model reaches maxToolRounds without producing text (line 183: !finalText && toolRounds >= maxToolRounds), a fallback message is added and another request is made. However, if that request also returns no text, finalText remains empty string (initialized line 126). This empty string is passed to parseCritiquesResponse (line 202), which will parse an empty string and return zero critiques rather than surfacing the failure. This silently loses critic feedback.
fix: Check if finalText is still empty after the fallback attempt and either: (a) log an error and return empty array explicitly, or (b) re-attempt with a simpler prompt or raise an error.
verify_note: The issue is real but low-impact: (1) It's already logged, so not truly "silent"; (2) Returning empty array is graceful and consistent with error handling elsewhere (line 67); (3) The scenario is edge-case - requires model to ignore explicit instruction after maxToolRounds. However, the reviewer is correct that better observability is possible. A more explicit check after line 199 (if (!finalText) logger.warn(...)) would improve debugging of LLM instruction-following failures.
verify_reason: The code at lines 183-202 in argue.ts exhibits a real but low-severity issue. When a model reaches maxToolRounds without producing text, the fallback message is sent (lines 184-198). If this fallback response also contains no text (i.e., resp.text is empty), line 199 assigns this empty string to finalText. This empty string is then passed to parseCritiquesResponse (line 202), which silently returns an empty array (parse.ts line 89: "if (!jsonMatch) return []"). While this result IS logged via logger.info (line 203-207) with critiqueCount: 0, and IS reported via progress events (line 76), the failure is not distinguished from successful parsing that yields zero critiques. The code treats parsing failure (no JSON found) identically to successful parsing of empty response, missing an opportunity to distinguish between "model produced valid empty response" vs "model failed to follow the explicit instruction 'Do not request any more tools' and produced no text at all".

title: validateApiKey may incorrectly return true for malformed API responses
detail: At line 282, the function checks `!data.error` to validate the OpenRouter API key. If the response JSON is missing the `error` property (e.g., due to unexpected API format change), `data.error` is undefined, and `!undefined` evaluates to true, incorrectly treating an invalid/unexpected response as a valid key. The code should explicitly check for the presence and value of the error property.
fix: Change `return !data.error;` to `return data.error === undefined || data.error === false;` or better yet, check for a known success indicator like `data.data` or `data.user`.
verify_note: Real defect in logic, but currently has zero impact: (1) the function is dead code, never invoked anywhere, (2) not part of the public API, (3) would only trigger on a specifically malformed API response (empty JSON object), and (4) if triggered, would merely treat an invalid response as valid rather than causing a security breach. The non-OpenRouter code path (line 288) uses HTTP status codes instead, which is more robust. If this function were ever reactivated, the fix would be to check for a known success indicator like `!data.error || data.data` rather than just `!data.error`.
verify_reason: The code at line 282 uses `return !data.error;` to validate OpenRouter API keys. This logic is flawed: if the API returns `{}` (no error property), `data.error` is undefined, and `!undefined` evaluates to true, causing the function to return true (valid key) for an unexpected/invalid response. However, this is currently dead code—the function is exported but never called in the codebase (grep for `validateApiKey(` returns only the definition at line 275, no callers). The function is also absent from the public API exports in index.ts.

title: Unhandled JSON/Zod parsing error in update.ts
detail: Line 26 calls JSON.parse() followed by CSMeta.parse() without try-catch. If the .cs-meta.json file is corrupted or has invalid structure, this will throw an unhandled exception, leaving the plugin in an unknown state. Line 35 has the same issue with CodexPluginManifest.parse().
fix: Wrap JSON.parse() and the Zod validation in a try-catch block, throwing PluginInstallError with a descriptive message on failure.
verify_note: The CLI layer (runGuarded) does catch all exceptions and exits cleanly, preventing user-facing crashes. However, this is incidental error handling at the caller level rather than proper error handling within the function itself. The function should wrap parsing operations in try-catch and convert SyntaxError/ZodError to PluginInstallError to match the established pattern in install.ts and the error contract implied by line 32's manual PluginInstallError throw. This is a correctness issue with low practical impact due to CLI-level error handling, but it violates API contract consistency and could cause problems for direct library users.
verify_reason: Lines 33 and 51 in update.ts call JSON.parse() and Zod validation (.parse()) without try-catch. If the .cs-meta.json file is malformed, JSON.parse throws SyntaxError. If the manifest JSON is invalid, it throws ZodError. These errors are not converted to PluginInstallError like the pattern established elsewhere in the codebase (see install.ts lines 40-84 which wraps parsing in try-catch). The errors bubble up as raw exceptions rather than the PluginInstallError that the API contract suggests callers should expect. While the CLI caller (plugin.ts) has runGuarded which catches all exceptions, library consumers calling updatePluginByName directly would receive unexpected exception types, violating error handling consistency.

title: SessionStatus includes 'paused' but it's not documented as a valid TerminalReason
detail: Line 101 defines SessionStatus as 'active' | 'paused' | TerminalReason. However, the JSDoc comment on lines 94-99 states: 'All other values match the {@link TerminalReason} the last run returned'. TerminalReason (lines 220-229) is a union of specific terminal states (completed, stop_hook_prevented, hook_stopped, prompt_too_long, model_error, aborted_streaming, aborted_tools, max_turns, image_error), but 'paused' is not among them. This violates the contract documented in the comment and creates ambiguity about what 'paused' represents and when it should be used.
fix: Either (1) add 'paused' to the TerminalReason union if it represents a valid terminal state, (2) update the JSDoc comment to clarify that 'paused' is a distinct state outside of TerminalReason, or (3) remove 'paused' from SessionStatus if it's not actually used.
verify_note: This is a documentation/type correctness issue rather than a runtime bug, since 'paused' is never actually used in the code. However, it creates maintainability confusion and violates the documented contract. The fix should either remove 'paused' from SessionStatus (most likely, since it's never set), update the JSDoc to acknowledge it as a distinct non-terminal state, or add it to TerminalReason if future pause functionality is intended.
verify_reason: SessionStatus includes 'paused' as a union member, but this value is not present in the TerminalReason union. The JSDoc comment states "All other values match the {@link TerminalReason} the last run returned", which creates a contract violation. More critically, 'paused' is never set anywhere in the codebase—SessionManager only sets status to "active", and engine.ts sets it to result.reason (which is always a TerminalReason value from turn-loop.ts). The value appears to be a vestigial remnant from the pre-refactoring design (commit 7f1e80b changed from "active|paused|completed|errored" to "active|paused|TerminalReason") that was not fully cleaned up.

title: Readline interface resource leak on child process exit
detail: A readline interface is created at line 103 with `createInterface({ input: this.child.stdout })` but never closed. When the child process exits at line 120, the readline interface remains open, leaking file descriptors. The `rl` variable goes out of scope without calling `rl.close()`, preventing proper cleanup of the underlying streams.
fix: Capture `rl` as an instance variable or call `rl.close()` in the 'exit' event handler at line 120 before setting `this.child = null`.
verify_note: This is a real but low-severity issue. While the code has a resource management defect (not explicitly closing the readline interface), the actual risk of file descriptor leaks is minimal because: (1) the child stdout stream closes when the child process exits, triggering automatic cleanup, and (2) spawnChild is idempotent and only creates one readline per child lifecycle. The issue is primarily a code quality concern and violation of Node.js best practices rather than a practical memory/FD leak threat. To fix: store `rl` and call `rl.close()` in the exit handler, or use `once('close')` on the readline to track when cleanup is complete.
verify_reason: The readline interface created at line 103 is never explicitly closed. While the variable `rl` goes out of scope after the method returns, the event listeners attached to it (line 104) maintain indirect references. When the child process exits at line 120, `this.child.stdout` will close, which should trigger automatic cleanup of the readline interface through Node.js stream handling. However, Node.js best practices recommend explicitly closing readline interfaces to ensure all associated resources and buffers are properly freed. The absence of an explicit `rl.close()` call in the exit handler violates resource management best practices, even though practical file descriptor leaks are unlikely in this case since the underlying stream eventually closes.
